findings.title: Admin password reset (POST /users/:id/reset-password) does not bump JWT nbf, leaving existing access tokens valid for up to 15 min
findings.description: UsersService.resetPassword (the admin path, reached via POST /users/:id/reset-password) calls refreshTokenService.revokeAllForUser to revoke all refresh tokens, but does NOT call jwtNotBefore.bumpUser. As a result, any live access token belonging to the target user continues to be valid until its natural TTL expires (default 15 minutes). The complementary path — AuthService.resetPassword (reached via POST /auth/reset-password with a token) — correctly calls BOTH revokeAllForUser AND jwtNotBefore.bumpUser (auth.service.ts line 585). The SEC-019 rationale is already documented in the codebase: 'without this bump a stolen access token stays valid until it expires (≤15 min) after the victim resets.'
findings.root_cause: jwtNotBefore.bumpUser was added to AuthService.resetPassword (SEC-019) but the equivalent admin path in UsersService.resetPassword was not updated.
findings.impact: After an admin force-resets a user's password (e.g. in response to credential compromise), the target user's existing access tokens remain usable for up to 15 minutes. An attacker who has stolen an access token can continue to act as that user despite the forced reset, until token TTL expires.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await this.jwtNotBefore.bumpUser(userId);` immediately after `await this.refreshTokenService.revokeAllForUser(userId);` in UsersService.resetPassword (around line 1130). JwtNotBeforeService is already injected in UsersService (line 62) so no additional wiring is needed.
acceptance_criteria: After POST /users/:id/reset-password, a previously-issued access token for the target user is rejected by JwtStrategy.validate on the next request.
The Redis key jwt:nbf:<userId> exists with a value equal to approximately now+1 (seconds) and TTL <= JWT_ACCESS_TTL + 60s.
Integration test: issue access token, admin-reset password, verify 401 on the old token within 1 second.
findings.notes: jwtNotBefore is already injected (constructor line 62, field declared at line 63). The fix is one line.

findings.title: LoginDto.password has no @MaxLength — long password input allows DoS via bcrypt CPU exhaustion
findings.description: The LoginDto's password field only enforces @IsString and @MinLength(6). There is no @MaxLength. bcrypt is deliberately CPU-intensive: hashing or comparing a very long string (e.g. 10 KB) can consume hundreds of milliseconds. Even with the @Throttle limit (5/min per IP) an attacker can send 5 requests per minute each with a 1 MB password body, generating significant CPU load. The per-IP throttle mitigates rate but not payload size. Many bcrypt libraries also truncate at 72 bytes, but the comparison still iterates on the full input up to that truncation in some implementations, and the server reads and deserialises the full body. NestJS's ValidationPipe does not cap body size by itself; a separate global limit should exist, but DTO-level MaxLength is the defense-in-depth layer at the validation gate.
findings.root_cause: LoginDto was never given a @MaxLength on password, and the global throttle was treated as the sole guard against credential-stuffing DoS.
findings.impact: A single attacker IP can send 5 requests/minute each with a multi-MB password string, causing high CPU load on the auth path. Under sustained attack from multiple IPs this degrades auth latency for legitimate users.
findings.suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength(1024) to LoginDto.password (and optionally @MaxLength(254) to login, matching the NIST recommendation). Also confirm that Fastify's bodyLimit is set to a reasonable value (e.g. 1 MB) in main.ts to cap the raw body size before DTO validation.
acceptance_criteria: POST /auth/login with a password longer than 1024 characters returns 400 Bad Request.
Login with a valid <= 1024 char password still succeeds.
findings.notes: bcrypt truncates at 72 bytes internally, but the body is still read and validated. MaxLength(1024) is a safe cap that doesn't break legitimate use.

findings.title: POST /users/:id/reset-password uses 'users:manage_roles' permission — semantically wrong and may grant unintended reset capability to role managers
findings.description: The admin password-reset endpoint at POST /users/:id/reset-password requires the permission 'users:manage_roles', not 'users:reset_password'. The complementary token-generation endpoint at POST /auth/reset-password-token uses 'users:reset_password'. This means that any role template carrying 'users:manage_roles' (role administration) can directly reset any subordinate user's password, even if the operator intended role administration and password reset to be separate privileges. Conversely, a role template that holds 'users:reset_password' but not 'users:manage_roles' cannot use this direct-reset endpoint, even though it can use the token-generation path. The inconsistency means the password-reset access surface is wider than explicitly designed: role managers gain implicit password-reset authority.
findings.root_cause: The direct-reset endpoint was wired to 'users:manage_roles' instead of the purpose-built 'users:reset_password' permission, possibly because it was added alongside role-management features.
findings.impact: Any role bound to a template that includes 'users:manage_roles' can directly reset subordinate users' passwords. If the operator assigns 'users:manage_roles' to a role they intend as pure role-administration (not credential management), they silently gain password-reset authority over subordinates. This widens the blast radius of a compromised account with role-management access.
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 431 to @RequirePermissions('users:reset_password'). Verify that all templates intended to perform admin password resets carry 'users:reset_password'. If both are needed, add @RequireAnyPermission('users:manage_roles', 'users:reset_password') with a comment explaining the dual need.
acceptance_criteria: A caller with only 'users:manage_roles' (and not 'users:reset_password') gets 403 on POST /users/:id/reset-password.
A caller with 'users:reset_password' gets 200 on POST /users/:id/reset-password (provided hierarchy check passes).
E2E: RBAC matrix confirms the reset-password endpoint is not reachable by REFERENT_TECHNIQUE roles that only hold manage_roles.
findings.notes: Both POST /auth/reset-password-token and POST /users/:id/reset-password protect against self-reset and enforce hierarchy in the service layer, so the guard mismatch does not enable privilege escalation — but it does widen the surface for who can reset passwords.

findings.title: CreateUserDto.login and ImportUserDto.login missing @MaxLength — unbounded login field
findings.description: CreateUserDto.login and ImportUserDto.login (same pattern at import-users.dto.ts lines 28-34) have @IsString and @MinLength(3) but no @MaxLength. The RegisterDto.login correctly has @MaxLength(50). Without a cap, an adversary with 'users:create' or 'users:import' can store an arbitrarily long login string in the DB. The DB column may have its own limit (likely varchar), but the DTO layer should enforce it independently for defense-in-depth and to produce a cleaner 400 rather than a DB-layer 500 on overflow.
findings.root_cause: RegisterDto was hardened with @MaxLength(50) but the same constraint was not copied to CreateUserDto and ImportUserDto when they were defined.
findings.impact: An admin or importer could create users with very long login strings, potentially causing DB truncation/errors or storage abuse. Low severity because the endpoint is admin-only (users:create / users:import).
findings.suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength(50) to CreateUserDto.login and ImportUserDto.login, matching RegisterDto.login.
acceptance_criteria: POST /users with a login longer than 50 characters returns 400 Bad Request.
POST /users/import with a login longer than 50 characters reports a per-row error, not a 500.
findings.notes: Minor: admin-only path, DB column size also limits in practice. But the DTO should enforce it.

findings.title: getUsersPresence accepts arbitrary dateStr with no format validation — invalid date causes NaN Date passed to $queryRaw
findings.description: The date query parameter is passed directly to new Date(dateStr) without validation. If dateStr is an invalid date string (e.g. 'not-a-date', 'NaN', ''), new Date() produces an Invalid Date object (whose getTime() returns NaN). This NaN Date is then passed as a bound parameter to the Prisma $queryRaw template literal. Prisma will serialize NaN/Invalid Date in a DB-driver-specific way: PostgreSQL will likely reject it with a type error (500 Internal Server Error), but the error message from AllExceptionsFilter is safely opaqued to 'Internal server error'. The path is: GET /users/presence?date=<badstring> → service crash → 500. This is a denial-of-service concern and a poor user experience. The SQL injection risk is nil because Prisma's tagged template literal parameterizes values.
findings.root_cause: The date query parameter is not validated before use — no regex or isValid() check, no DTO validator.
findings.impact: Any authenticated user with 'users:read' can trigger a 500 by passing an invalid date string to GET /users/presence. This is a minor DoS/observability issue.
findings.suggested_fix: In the controller, add a query parameter validator: either use a custom pipe (@Query('date', new ParseDatePipe())) or add a guard in the service: `if (dateStr && isNaN(new Date(dateStr).getTime())) throw new BadRequestException('Date format invalide — use YYYY-MM-DD');`
findings.notes: No injection risk — Prisma parameterizes. Primarily a robustness/DoS concern that leaks a 500 to authenticated users.

findings.title: uploads-auth.hook.ts skips jti blacklist and nbf checks — logged-out tokens remain valid for avatar reads up to access TTL
findings.description: The uploads auth hook (SEC-016) only checks that the Bearer token's signature is valid and has not expired — it does NOT check the jti blacklist (JwtBlacklistService) or the per-user nbf (JwtNotBeforeService). The comment in the file acknowledges this intentionally: 'a logged-out-but-unexpired token revealing a profile photo for ≤ the access TTL is negligible vs. the anonymous hole'. However, this also means that after a forced password reset or account deactivation (both of which bump nbf via JwtNotBeforeService), the old token can still be used to retrieve avatar images for the remaining access TTL. For a public-sector app handling HR data (org chart avatars could be sensitive), this could be a privacy concern. The mitigating factors are: (1) avatarUrl is already returned to every authenticated user in summaries, so avatars are not confidential; (2) the TTL gap is ≤15 min.
findings.root_cause: Intentional design decision documented in the hook's comment — the blacklist/nbf checks were omitted as disproportionate for avatar access.
findings.impact: A logged-out user (via /auth/logout) or a deactivated/reset user can continue to fetch avatar images for up to JWT_ACCESS_TTL after the security event. Low severity because avatars are already visible to all authenticated users in summaries.
findings.suggested_fix: Accept as intentional, or optionally add a lightweight blacklist check by injecting JwtBlacklistService into the hook factory: `if (payload.jti && await blacklist.isBlacklisted(payload.jti)) return unauthorized(reply);`. This adds one Redis round-trip per avatar fetch but closes the logout-bypass for avatar reads.
acceptance_criteria: After POST /auth/logout, GET /api/uploads/avatars/<id>.jpg with the old token returns 401.
findings.notes: Documented intentional decision; the operator should confirm the avatar-confidentiality level for their deployment.

verified_clean: auth/auth.controller.ts: all routes have @Public, @AllowSelfService, or @RequirePermissions; throttle applied to all public auth routes

auth/auth.service.ts: no hardcoded secrets; password compared via bcrypt; validateUser returns null uniformly on bad password and disabled account (SEC-005 timing collapse); reset token stored as SHA-256 hash, not plaintext; AUTH_EXPOSE_RESET_TOKEN gated to non-production only
auth/dto/login.dto.ts: @IsString, @MinLength on both fields; no free-form HTML fields
auth/dto/register.dto.ts: @IsEmail, @IsStrongPassword, @MaxLength on all fields; @Matches(NO_CONTROL_CHARS) on name fields; registration gated by REGISTRATION_ENABLED env flag
auth/dto/reset-password.dto.ts: @IsString on token, @IsStrongPassword on newPassword
auth/dto/reset-password-token.dto.ts: @IsUUID on userId — prevents non-UUID injection
auth/dto/refresh-token.dto.ts: @IsOptional + @IsString; no injection risk
auth/guards/jwt-auth.guard.ts: properly skips @Public routes and delegates to passport-jwt
auth/guards/force-password-change.guard.ts: reads DB-live forcePasswordChange flag, not stale JWT claim
auth/guards/throttler-behind-proxy.guard.ts: uses clientIp() — correct IP extraction behind nginx proxy
auth/strategies/jwt.strategy.ts: checks jti blacklist then nbf before DB lookup; rejects inactive users; attaches jti+exp for logout blacklisting
auth/strategies/local.strategy.ts: delegates to authService.validateUser; no direct DB access
auth/jwt-blacklist.service.ts: fail-CLOSED on Redis read error; fail-CLOSED on write error (throws 503); proper TTL expiry
auth/jwt-not-before.service.ts: correct unit handling (all seconds); +1 same-second guard; fail-OPEN on read (acceptable — blacklist checked first)
auth/login-lockout.service.ts: per-(account,IP) keying via SHA-256 hash (no raw identifier in Redis); progressive lockout with cap; fail-OPEN documented
auth/refresh-token.service.ts: tokens stored as SHA-256 hash; serializable transaction on rotate; reuse detection revokes all tokens for user; randomBytes(48) entropy
auth/auth.module.ts: correct APP_GUARD order — ThrottlerBehindProxyGuard → JwtAuthGuard → ForcePasswordChangeGuard

rbac/permissions.guard.ts: PermissionsGuardV2 is zero-trust in enforce mode; @Public, @AllowSelfService, @RequirePermissions, @RequireAnyPermission all handled; fail-closed on uncovered routes in enforce mode
rbac/permissions.service.ts: RBAC V4 compile-time via ROLE_TEMPLATES; Redis cache with negative-TTL; singleflight coalescing; no raw SQL
rbac/roles.controller.ts: all endpoints require 'users:manage_roles'; ParseUUIDPipe on :id params
rbac/roles.service.ts: isSystem=true roles are immutable (403 on update/delete); templateKey immutable after creation; all Prisma queries parameterized
rbac/dto/create-role.dto.ts: @IsString, @MinLength, @MaxLength, @Matches(SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE) on code; @IsIn(ROLE_TEMPLATE_KEYS) on templateKey
rbac/dto/update-role.dto.ts: only label/description/isDefault editable; templateKey excluded from DTO
users/users.controller.ts: all routes have @RequirePermissions or @AllowSelfService; ParseUUIDPipe on all :id params; avatar upload is throttled

users/users.service.ts: horizontal scope enforced via accessScope.assertCanManageUser on update/remove; role hierarchy enforced via roleHierarchy.assertCanAssignRole on all role assignments; bcrypt cost=12; no $queryRawUnsafe or string-concatenated SQL; $queryRaw uses parameterized template literals; avatar path validated via AVATAR_URL_PATTERN before DB write; magic-byte check via assertMagicBytes; avatar filename constructed server-side from userId, never from user-supplied filename

users/dto/create-user.dto.ts: @IsEmail, @MaxLength(254) on email; @IsUUID on departmentId and serviceIds; @Matches(AVATAR_URL_PATTERN) on avatarUrl; @IsStrongPassword on password; isActive not in CreateUserDto (SEC-011)
users/dto/update-user.dto.ts: extends CreateUserDto via PartialType; isActive explicitly declared with @IsBoolean; password @IsStrongPassword
users/dto/change-password.dto.ts: @IsString on currentPassword, @IsStrongPassword on newPassword
users/dto/reset-password.dto.ts (users): @IsStrongPassword on newPassword
users/dto/import-users.dto.ts: @IsEmail, @MaxLength(254) on email; @IsStrongPassword on password; @ValidateNested + @Type for nested validation
users/dto/avatar-preset.dto.ts: @IsIn(VALID_PRESETS) — whitelist-only validation
common/fastify/cors.config.ts: production boot-assert if CORS_ORIGIN not set; no wildcard fallback in prod
common/fastify/trust-proxy.config.ts: ['loopback','uniquelocal'] — most restrictive safe config for nginx-in-Docker topology
common/fastify/redact.config.ts: authorization, cookie, password fields, refreshToken, access_token, passwordHash all redacted from Fastify logs
common/fastify/request-id.context.ts: x-request-id header capped to 128 chars, CR/LF stripped — log injection prevention
common/fastify/uploads-auth.hook.ts: authentication gate on /api/uploads/* before static handler; OPTIONS preflight correctly allowed
common/filters/all-exceptions.filter.ts: non-HttpException errors return opaque 500 (no stack/message leakage); request ID threaded for correlation
common/guards/ownership.guard.ts: requires authenticated user; reads resource ID from route params; bypassPermission checked via PermissionsService
common/services/access-scope.service.ts: management-tier bypass is permission-driven (users:manage), not role-code; horizontal scope enforced for findOne/getUsersBy*
common/services/ownership.service.ts: all lookups select only ownership field; returns false on not-found (404/403 decision left to caller)
common/services/role-hierarchy.service.ts: ADMIN template bypass explicit; callerRank must be strictly greater than targetRank; no hardcoded role codes in business logic
common/upload/magic-bytes.validator.ts: file-type library used for magic-byte check; MIME allowlist per upload kind; rejection on unknown type
common/validators/password-policy.ts: single source of truth for password policy; min 8 chars + uppercase + digit + special char; imperative validatePasswordStrength for non-DTO paths
common/config/jwt-secret.ts: JWT_SECRET length >= 32 enforced at boot in production
common/config/audit-hash-key.ts: AUDIT_HASH_KEY >= 32 chars enforced at boot in ALL environments
prisma/prisma.service.ts: cleanDatabase() gated to non-production; no raw SQL in service; slow query logging at 200ms threshold
common/error-reporter.ts: no external egress in default reporter; unhandledRejection and uncaughtException handled
